title: Red Card Overturned — Wrongful Dismissal
summary: A player received a straight red card for a tackle that was later proven to be a fair challenge with no contact.
match: Brazil vs Croatia — Group A (Opening Match)
description: In the opening match of the 2014 World Cup, a Brazilian player made a tackle in midfield that the referee deemed serious foul play, producing a straight red card. Replays showed the Brazilian player had won the ball cleanly — his foot made contact only with the ball, and there was no follow-through, no studs showing, and no excessive force. The decision effectively reduced Brazil to 10 men for the final 20 minutes of their World Cup opener. The red card was later heavily criticised by pundits and was described as one of the worst decisions of the tournament. FIFA's own disciplinary committee declined to extend the suspension upon review, implicitly acknowledging the error.

videos.title: Red Card Tackle — Slow Motion Analysis

images.caption: The tackle clearly wins the ball with no contact on the opponent.

rules.explanation: Law 12 defines sending-off offences, including 'serious foul play' which is defined as a tackle that 'endangers the safety of an opponent' or uses 'excessive force' or 'brutality.' A tackle that wins the ball cleanly, with no contact on the opponent and no excessive force, does not meet any of these criteria. The referee's decision was a clear misapplication of Law 12.

Law 5.3 grants the referee authority to 'take disciplinary action against players guilty of cautionable and sending-off offences.' However, this discretion must be exercised 'according to the Laws of the Game.' Issuing a red card for a fair tackle that won the ball cleanly falls outside this authority and constitutes a serious error in the application of the Laws.
